intro: 1985. Intel invented the memory chip business and it's being annihilated — Japanese manufacturers are dumping DRAM below cost and you're losing money on every chip. Memory is your identity. You run Intel, and your president just asked a dangerous question.

realOutcome.summary: Intel walked away from the business it invented and became the king of microprocessors. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: Andy Grove turns to Gordon Moore and asks: if the board fired us and brought in a new CEO, what would he do? The answer is obvious and unbearable — get out of memory, the business that made Intel.

choices.text: Exit DRAM memory entirely. Walk out that door.
choices.reveal: Grove and Moore did exactly this — they acted as if new leadership had walked in and killed the memory business. Abandoning Intel's founding product freed it to bet on something bigger.

choices.text: Keep fighting in memory. It's who we are.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You defend an identity, not a business. The Japanese keep dumping, the losses compound, and Intel bleeds out defending a commodity it can't win.

choices.text: Merge with a Japanese rival to survive memory.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You chain yourself to a race to the bottom. Memory becomes a commodity worth pennies, and the microprocessor future belongs to someone braver.

decisions.scenario: If you leave memory, you need a new bet-the-company product. The microprocessor — the 386 — is promising but unproven as the core of the whole business.

choices.text: Go all-in on microprocessors and the 386.
choices.reveal: Intel bet everything on the microprocessor. The 386 and its successors became the brain of the PC revolution and one of the most profitable products in history.

choices.text: Hedge across many small product lines.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You spread thin to feel safe and master nothing. Focus was the whole point; a portfolio of hedges never becomes the standard the industry is built on.

choices.text: Become a contract fab, building others' chips.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You give up designing the product and just manufacture other people's ideas. You surrender the architecture that would have been worth hundreds of billions.

decisions.scenario: The pivot demands sacrifice. To fund the microprocessor bet and stop the memory losses, you'd have to lay off roughly a third of the company.

choices.text: Make the deep cuts to fund the pivot.
choices.reveal: Intel made painful, deep layoffs to survive the transition. It was brutal, but it freed the capital and focus to win microprocessors decisively.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You spare jobs and starve the new bet. A slow pivot in a fast industry means you arrive at the microprocessor party after it's over.

choices.text: Freeze everything and wait for the market to turn.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The memory market does not turn in your favor. Waiting just spends the runway you needed for the real fight.

decisions.scenario: Your chips are hidden inside other companies' computers. You could try something no component maker has done: brand the part itself directly to consumers, so people ask for it by name.

choices.reveal: The 'Intel Inside' campaign, launched in 1991, made buyers demand a specific invisible component. It turned a commodity part into a premium brand and gave Intel pricing power for decades.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You remain an anonymous supplier at the mercy of PC makers' purchasing departments. No brand means no premium and no leverage.

choices.text: Market only to PC manufacturers, not consumers.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You court the buyers instead of creating consumer pull. Without end users demanding Intel by name, you're just one of several interchangeable chip vendors.

decisions.scenario: A subtle math error is discovered in your flagship Pentium chip. It affects almost no one in practice, but it's spreading online and the press is circling. A full recall would cost a fortune.

choices.text: Do a full, no-questions recall — whatever it costs.
choices.reveal: After initially resisting, Intel offered a full no-questions replacement and took a ~$475M charge on the Pentium FDIV bug. The trust it bought was worth far more than the money it cost.

choices.text: Replace chips only for 'genuinely affected' users.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You make customers prove they deserve a fix. The condescension becomes the story, and the brand you spent years building takes the damage instead of the chip.

choices.text: Deny the bug matters and wait it out.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Stonewalling turns a minor flaw into a referendum on your honesty. In the new online era, that's a fire you can't put out with a press release.

bands.line: You killed your own founding business and won the future. Legend episode: WON.
A solid chip company — but not the brain of the whole PC era.
You died defending the memory business. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Intel 12x'd by abandoning its own invention. Today's agents flagged a company clinging to a dying core →
